title: Interpretable Deep Learning for EEG Decoding and Analysis of Emotions
author: Borra, Davide
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Electroencephalography (EEG) is widely used in affective computing and affective neuroscience because it provides a non-invasive measure of fast brain dynamics associated with emotional experience. Deep neural networks can improve EEG-based emotion decoding by learning discriminative representations directly from raw or minimally pre-processed data, but their limited interpretability can hinder their use for analyzing the neural signatures of emotion. This chapter presents a reproducible computational protocol for interpretable deep learning-based emotion decoding and analysis from EEG signals. The protocol combines EEG decoding with complementary interpretability strategies, allowing trained EEG decoders to be used not only for classification but also as nonlinear tools for investigating emotion-related EEG patterns in an automatic, end-to-end, and data-driven manner. The approach is illustrated on the DEAP dataset using a within-subject binary valence classification task. First, the chapter introduces EEG emotion decoding using a deep neural network (EEGNet). Second, it introduces two complementary interpretability strategies: network decision analysis using a post-hoc attribution method (DeepLIFT) to estimate channel-level contributions to model decisions, and network feature analysis, promoted by ad-hoc interpretable modelling, to derive class-discriminative representations of spectral-spatial features. Overall, the protocol provides practical guidance for applying interpretable deep learning to EEG-based emotion decoding and for exploring data-driven EEG signatures associated with emotional states.
